feedback.text: Blank, I am writing to provide feedback on the Digital Fairness Act. First of all, I am happy to note the attempt to stop the most harmful practices such as dark patterns. As some of you have already noticed, the European Stop Killing Games initiative has exceeded the quota of one million and four hundred thousand signatures. The reason is quite simple: Increasingly publishers/developers are leaving products for which people have paid in an inaccessible state after they cease to be profitable. This is a highly unfair practice, as consumers are thus deprived of the good they have purchased. Suffice it to see The Crew of Ubisoft, or, again by Ubisoft, Xdefiant, two games with extra content that can be bought at stake, which have been rendered inaccessible, without an End of Life function allowing consumers to access, albeit in a limited or unaided manner by the publisher, their product or the content purchased at stake. The Digital Fairness Act therefore requires developers and publishers to implement solutions (e.g. offline, community-operated private server tools, or limited source code releases) that guarantee the usability of digital products purchased even after the official support has ceased. I would also like to draw attention to the recent Visa/Mastercard scandal, which forced Steam and Itch.Io platforms to remove various games for which customers have paid, in this case it is not so much the problem for the type of product, which may be more or less unthinkable, but the fact that companies primarily involved in the management of transactions are not entitled to interfere with consumer purchases and therefore calls for the Digital Fairness Act to include clear provisions limiting the ability of payment intermediaries to unilaterally require the removal of lawfully sold and purchased digital products, thus safeguarding both consumer rights and the sustainability of publishers, in particular independent publishers. I hope this feedback will help to further strengthen the Digital Fairness Act, making it an even more effective tool to protect consumers and promote a fair and free digital ecosystem. Thank you for your attention and commitment.
